column2: Description (This field is optional, you can leave it blank if you want to.)
column3: Document Content (Mandatory, set one of the following values: 0 for Use Content, 1 for Use Attachments, 2 for Use URL)
column4: Content Editor Text (This field is mandatory only if Document Content is set to "Use Content".)
column5: URL (This field is mandatory only if Document Content is set to "URL".)
column6: GRC Contact (Mandatory. Accepts multiple user logins or group names separated by "|". For User login use prefix "User-" and for Group name use "Group-". For example "User-admin|Group-Third Party Feedback|Group-Admin". You can get the login of an user account from System / Settings / User Management or name of a group from System / Settings / Groups.)
column7: Policy Reviewer Contact (Mandatory. Accepts multiple user logins or group names separated by "|". For User login use prefix "User-" and for Group name use "Group-". For example "User-admin|Group-Third Party Feedback|Group-Admin". You can get the login of an user account from System / Settings / User Management or name of a group from System / Settings / Groups.)
column8: Tags (Optional and accepts tags separated by "|". For example "Critical|SOX|PCI".)
column9: Published Date (Mandatory, it must follow the format YYYY-MM-DD, not the "-" character is used as delimiter.)
column10: Next Review Date (Mandatory, it must follow the format YYYY-MM-DD, not the "-" character is used as delimiter.)
column11: Policy Labels (Optional, set the value of the name of the label you want to use. Label names can be obtained from Asset Management / Asset Identification / Settings / Labels.)
column12: Related Projects (Optional and accepts multiple names separated by "|". You need to enter the name of a project, you can find them at Security Operations / Project Management.)
column13: Status (Mandatory, set value: 0 for Draft, 1 for Published)
column14: Document Type (You need to provide the name of the policy type, you can obtain the name of type from Control Catalogue / Policies / Settings / Document Type.)
column16: Policy Portal Permissions for this Document (Mandatory, set one of the following values: public for Public (Everyone can see the document), private for Private (Document is not shown on the portal), custom-roles for Only specific groups)
column17: Custom Roles (Mandatory in case you set permission to custom-roles, you can set more of following values: Owners for GRC Contact, Collaborators for Policy Reviewer Contact)
column18: Related Documents (Optional and accepts multiple names separated by "|". You need to enter the name of a policy, you can find them at Control Catalogue / Policies.)

column4:
Introduction


The Access Management Policy defines the organization's approach to ensuring appropriate access controls on all systems, including the full lifecycle management of identities and secure authentication technologies. Proper management and regular review of access rights, along with robust authentication methods, ensure the organization's security and compliance with industry standards, ensuring accountability and proper identity management.

Objectives


• Establish standards for access control and secure authentication, ensuring only authorized personnel can access systems and data.
• Define the roles of System Owners, IT, and Management in overseeing access controls and implementing secure authentication technologies.
• Implement procedures for account provisioning, de-provisioning, changes in access rights, managing the full lifecycle of identities, and secure authentication practices.
• Achieve consistent compliance with organizational standards for access management and authentication across all systems, managing identities assigned to both persons and non-human entities.
• Control and monitor the use of utility programs capable of overriding system and application controls, ensuring their use is restricted to authorized and qualified personnel.

Accounts & Roles Standard


• Systems must follow the guidelines defined in this policy, including identity lifecycle management and secure authentication measures.
• Implement multi-factor authentication for accessing critical information systems, using a combination of multiple factors such as what you know (passwords), what you have (tokens, smart cards), and what you are (biometrics).
• Ensure biometric authentication is accompanied by at least one alternative authentication technique and is invalidated if compromised.
• Design log-on procedures to minimize the risk of unauthorized access, incorporating measures like CAPTCHA, alerting system administrators of potential breaches, and logging both unsuccessful and successful attempts.
• Secure the transmission of authentication information, avoiding the display of passwords in clear text, and preventing their transmission in clear text over networks.
• Implement session termination after a defined period of inactivity, especially in high-risk locations.
• Limit the use of utility programs to a minimum number of trusted, authorized users, employing unique identification and stringent authorization procedures.
• Ensure utility programs are not accessible to users where segregation of duties is required, removing or disabling all unnecessary utility programs.
• Logically segregate utility programs from application software, and where practical, segregate their network communications from application traffic.
• Limit the availability of utility programs, such as for the duration of an authorized change, and log all uses of these programs.

Account Provisioning


• Access and identity assignment should be requested via a ticketing system, providing a rationale for the necessity.
• System Owners and IT have the primary role in reviewing and granting these requests, ensuring compliance with the Access Control Matrix and identity management standards.
• Proper documentation of decisions on access and identity assignment requests ensures transparency and accountability.
• Third-party access and identities are controlled and monitored, ensuring contractual agreements like NDAs are in place and the third-party identities provide the required trust level.
• Provisioning of access rights includes obtaining authorization from the owner of the information and other associated assets.
• The roles of approval and implementation of access rights are segregated to avoid conflicts of interest.
• Define and document authorization levels for utility programs, ensuring they align with the organization’s broader access control policies and standards.

Account De-Provisioning


• Removal of access rights and identities also follows a ticketing process.
• Reviews and decisions on de-provisioning are jointly handled by the System Owner, IT, and Management.
• Regular review of access rights and identities is essential to revoke unnecessary privileges and to ensure no duplicate identities exist within the same domain.
• In addition to the existing process, ensure timely removal of access rights for users who have left the organization or changed roles.
• Temporary access rights for a limited time period should be considered, especially for temporary personnel or access required by personnel, and revoked at the expiration date.

Secure Authentication Standards


• Choose suitable authentication techniques based on the classification of the information to be accessed.
• Protect against brute force log-on attempts, requiring password reset after a predefined number of failed attempts, or blocking the user after a maximum number of errors.
• Display a general notice warning that systems, applications, or services should only be accessed by authorized users and avoid providing help messages during the log-on procedure that could aid an unauthorized user.
• Ensure the log-on information is validated only on completion of all input data and do not display sensitive system or application information until the log-on process has been successfully completed.
• Display or send information on a separate channel on completion of a successful log-on, including the date and time of the previous successful log-on and details of any unsuccessful log-on attempts since then.
• Restrict connection duration times to provide additional security for high-risk applications and reduce the window of opportunity for unauthorized access.

Changes to the Access Control Matrix


• The Access Control Matrix's evolution needs the input and approval of the System Owner, IT, and Management, including changes related to identity management.
• Documentation of changes and reasons ensures transparency in both access control and identity management.
• IT is responsible for applying the changes, and the Access Control Matrix must be updated to reflect changes in identities as well.

Monitoring and Review


• System Owners play a crucial role in ensuring access control compliance, identity management, and reporting any discrepancies.
• Include authorizations for privileged access rights and make necessary adjustments based on risk evaluation.
• Maintain a central record of access rights granted to a user identifier (ID, logical or physical) to access information and other associated assets, along with changes made to these rights.
• IT's regular audits maintain the sanctity of the Access Control Matrix and the overall access control environment, including the management of user identities.
• Regularly audit the use of privileged utility programs, ensuring compliance with the policy's guidelines and identifying any unauthorized or inappropriate use.
